algorithms.title: Categorizing and Managing Pediatric Respiratory Compromise
algorithms.description: Field approach to categorizing a respiratory complaint by mechanism and matching treatment accordingly.
steps: Complete the PAT and primary assessment to identify a respiratory problem.

Listen and look for the category-defining pattern: stridor (upper airway), wheeze with prolonged expiration (lower airway), grunting/crackles/diminished sounds (lung tissue), or irregular pattern without classic sounds (disordered control).
For upper airway obstruction: keep the child calm, position of comfort with caregiver, minimize agitation, avoid invasive exam of the airway in the field.
For lower airway disease: consider nebulized bronchodilator per local protocol, position of comfort, monitor for fatigue.
For lung tissue disease: supplemental oxygen, position of comfort, monitor closely for hypoxia progression.
For disordered control of breathing: identify and address the underlying cause where possible (e.g., naloxone for suspected opioid exposure per protocol), support ventilation as needed.
Reassess PAT and vital signs frequently for any transition from distress toward failure.

algorithms.title: Recognizing and Responding to Impending Respiratory Failure
algorithms.description: Sequence for identifying early warning signs of failure and escalating support before deterioration is severe.
steps: Reassess a child in respiratory distress at frequent intervals, not just once.
Watch for new or worsening grunting.
Watch for decreasing work of breathing in a child who was previously struggling hard — treat this as a sign of exhaustion, not improvement, until proven otherwise.
Watch for a shift in PAT Appearance from anxious/agitated to lethargic.
Watch for a falling or irregular respiratory rate after a period of tachypnea.
If any of these signs are present, escalate immediately: assist ventilation with BVM and high-flow oxygen, prepare for rapid transport.
Do not wait for SpO2 to fall before escalating support — treat these clinical signs as leading indicators.
For drug-assisted airway management or arrest-specific ventilation parameters, refer to the PALS reviewer.
